does: This paper develops a physics-informed XGBoost model for predicting low-visibility and precipitation events at aviation sites using only METAR surface observation data. The model incorporates physics-guided feature engineering based on thermodynamic principles to capture local physical processes automatically. Evaluated across 11 international airports spanning diverse climatic regimes from 2000-2024, the lightweight framework achieves substantially improved detection rates compared to operational TAF forecasts at 3-hour nowcasting horizons, demonstrating 2.5 to 4.0 times higher recall while maintaining computational efficiency. The approach avoids the computational intensity and conservative biases characteristic of traditional numerical weather prediction guidance.
connects: This work builds on the established use of machine learning for weather prediction, particularly gradient boosting methods applied to aviation meteorology. It connects to research combining physics-based domain knowledge with data-driven models—a line of work addressing the gap between black-box machine learning and the interpretability and sample efficiency of physics-informed approaches. The paper responds to longstanding limitations of operational TAF products and NWP systems in nowcasting, which struggle with temporal resolution and systematic biases. It extends prior studies using METAR-only approaches by systematizing thermodynamic feature engineering rather than relying on manual configuration, and demonstrates generalization across diverse climatic regimes—an important extension beyond single-airport or single-climate studies.
enables: This framework enables more accurate and responsive aviation safety operations by providing probabilistic visibility and precipitation guidance at tactical timescales without heavy computational overhead, potentially reducing conservative forecast biases that currently constrain airport operations. The physics-guided feature engineering approach provides a template for incorporating domain knowledge into automated forecasting systems across other meteorological phenomena. The multi-airport, multi-climate evaluation opens pathways for global deployment of adaptive nowcasting systems tuned to local thermodynamic regimes. Future work could integrate additional data streams beyond METAR, apply the framework to other weather hazards, and investigate how automated systems could supplement or replace subjective human forecasting in operational contexts while maintaining safety margins.
